items.title: Flu vs. A cold
items.contentHtml:
Clinical differential diagnosis

  • Influenza: sudden onset (<12 h), fever >38.5°C, intense headache, myalgia, dry cough and odynophagia; febrile duration 3-5 days.
  • Common cold: gradual onset, feverish or low-grade fever, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, sneezing; Minimal or absent systemic symptoms.
  • In flu the fever is constant and high; In colds there is no significant affectation of the general condition.
  • Key difference: myalgia and fever >38°C point to flu; Absence of fever rules out influenza in >90% of immunocompetent adults.

items.title: Flu complications
items.contentHtml:
Most frequent complications

  • Primary viral pneumonia: bilateral interstitial infiltrates, hypoxemia, rapid evolution (48-72 hours).
  • Secondary bacterial pneumonia: due to Streptococcus pneumoniae, Staphylococcus aureus or Haemophilus influenzae; It appears after initial improvement (5-10 days), with recurrent fever and lobar consolidation.
  • Exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease or asthma, myocarditis, rhabdomyolysis and Reye's syndrome (associated with salicylates in children).
  • In children: acute otitis media and laryngotracheobronchitis.

items.title: Antivirals in Flu
items.contentHtml:
Indications for antivirals

  • Neuraminidase inhibitors (oseltamivir, zanamivir) indicated if symptoms begin <48 hours, in: hospitalized, severe or progressive disease, risk groups (pregnant women, immunocompromised, age >65 years, chronic diseases).
  • Dose: oseltamivir 75 mg every 12 hours for 5 days; in renal failure (glomerular filtration <30 ml/min) adjust to 75 mg/day.
  • Chemoprophylaxis: oseltamivir 75 mg/day for 10 days after exposure in high-risk unvaccinated people.
  • Not indicated in the common cold (different viral etiology: rhinovirus, coronavirus, adenovirus).

items.title: Symptomatic management
items.contentHtml:
Symptomatic treatment and antipyretics

  • First line antipyretic/analgesic: paracetamol 500-1000 mg every 6-8 h (max 4 g/day) or ibuprofen 400-600 mg every 6-8 h (max 2400 mg/day).
  • Avoid acid acetylsalicylic in children and adolescents due to the risk of Reye syndrome.
  • For dry cough: dextromethorphan 15-30 mg every 6-8 hours; antitussives not recommended for productive cough.
  • Nasal decongestants: topical oxymetazoline (max 3 days) or oral pseudoephedrine 60 mg every 4-6 hours.
  • Hydration and rest as the basis of treatment.

items.title: Flu vaccine
items.contentHtml:
Prevention with annual vaccine

  • Tetravalent inactivated vaccine (split virus or subunits) recommended annually for all people ≥6 months without contraindication.
  • Efficacy variable (40-60%) according to antigenic concordance; reduces hospitalization and death in risk groups.
  • Absolute contraindication: anaphylaxis to previous dose or components (egg in vaccines derived from chicken embryos; there are egg-free vaccines).
  • Optimal time: before the start of the season (autumn in the northern hemisphere), but it can be administered throughout the entire viral circulation.
  • Does not protect against the common cold.

items.title: Vulnerable population
items.contentHtml:
Risk groups for complications

  • Age <5 years (especially <2 years) and >65 years.
  • Pregnant and up to 2 weeks postpartum.
  • Chronic diseases: heart disease, lung disease (asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease), diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney failure, liver disease, hemoglobinopathy, immunosuppression (HIV/AIDS, chemotherapy, chronic corticosteroids).
  • Morbid obesity (body mass index ≥40 kg/m²).
  • Residents of chronic care centers.

items.title: When to admit
items.contentHtml:
Hospitalization criteria

  • Respiratory failure: oxygen saturation <93% in room air or respiratory rate >30 breaths/minute.
  • Arterial hypotension (systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg) or signs of shock.
  • Altered state of consciousness (confusion, drowsiness).
  • Severe dehydration (inability to drink fluids, oliguria).
  • Exacerbation of comorbidities not controllable at home.
  • Children: lethargy, subcostal indrawing, refusal of food, seizures.

items.title: Start: abrupt vs. gradual
items.contentHtml:
Differences in onset of symptoms

  • Flu: sudden onset (hours), with high fever, chills, headache and myalgia; the patient remembers the exact time of onset.
  • Common cold: gradual onset (1-3 days), with nasal symptoms (rhinorrhea, sneezing) preceding odynophagia; absent or mild fever.
  • In flu, the incubation period is 1-4 days (average 2 days); with a cold, 1-3 days.
  • The flu prodrome includes a feeling of prostration and retro-ocular pain.

items.title: Temporal evolution
items.contentHtml:
Typical duration of symptoms

  • Uncomplicated flu: fever persists for 3-5 days; respiratory symptoms and cough may last 1-2 weeks; residual asthenia up to 3 weeks.
  • Common cold: maximum symptoms after 2-3 days, total duration 7-10 days; Rhinorrhea and nasal congestion usually resolve within 1 week.
  • In influenza, cough and fatigue are the symptoms that resolve later.
  • If the fever persists >5 days or reappears, suspect a bacterial complication.

items.title: Diagnostic tests
items.contentHtml:
Rapid diagnostic tests

  • Rapid antigen detection tests (immunochromatography): sensitivity 50-70%, specificity >90%; result in 15-30 minutes.
  • Reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) is the gold standard: sensitivity >95%, allows subtyping; result in 1-4 hours.
  • Direct immunofluorescence: sensitivity 80-90%, requires nasopharyngeal aspirate or swab sample.
  • Main indication: hospitalized patients or patients with suspected influenza in an epidemic context to guide use of antivirals and isolation.

items.title: Avoid unnecessary antibiotics
items.contentHtml:
Rational use of antibiotics

  • Not indicated for flu or the common cold due to their viral etiology (influenza, rhinovirus, coronavirus, respiratory syncytial virus).
  • Only prescribe if there is evidence of bacterial superinfection: secondary bacterial pneumonia, acute otitis media, bacterial sinusitis (symptoms >10 days or biphasic worsening).
  • In secondary bacterial pneumonia post-flu, cover Streptococcus pneumoniae, Staphylococcus aureus and Haemophilus influenzae (e.g. amoxicillin-clavulanic acid or third-generation cephalosporin).
  • Use of biomarkers (procalcitonin) can help decide if there is a bacterial infection.

items.title: Transmission control
items.contentHtml:
Isolation and contagion measures

  • Isolation by drops for 5-7 days from the onset of symptoms or up to 24 hours after resolution of fever without antipyretics.
  • Surgical mask for the patient; frequent hand hygiene; avoid close contact (<1.5 meters).
  • In hospitals: single room with negative pressure if there is a risk of aerosols (intubation, bronchoscopy).
  • Infectious period: 1 day before the onset of symptoms until 5-7 days later; In immunocompromised people it can last weeks.
  • Common cold: maximum contagion first 2-3 days; not strict isolation, but recommend respiratory etiquette.
